title: Repairing a leather tear with saddle stitch
subtitle: Close a clean tear or split in a leather item using a hand saddle stitch
excerpt: How to close a straight tear in a leather bag, belt, or saddle flap using a hand saddle stitch, covering pre-punching the stitch line, waxing the thread, working the two-needle stitch, and finishing the thread ends.
sourceNotes: The Practical Upholsterer (1917). Cassell's Household Guide (leatherwork). W.H. Sutherland The Art of Leatherwork (1926).

glossaryTerms.definition: A hand stitching method using two needles on a single length of thread, one at each end. Each needle passes through the same hole from opposite sides, locking the stitch. Unlike a machine lock stitch, a saddle stitch does not unravel when one section breaks: each stitch is independently locked. The traditional stitch for leather harness, bags, and belts.

A multi-pronged metal tool that marks evenly spaced holes along a stitch line on leather. Tapped along the seam edge with a mallet, it both marks the positions and partially pierces the leather. Available in 3 mm, 4 mm, and 5 mm spacings. Roo and Mac tools are the leatherwork standard.

content.text: A clean tear in leather can be stitched closed with a
. The stitch line is punched with a

before stitching, which gives regular spacing and partially pierces the leather so the needles pass through cleanly. A saddle stitch is stronger than a machine stitch of the same thread on thick leather because the thread locks at every hole rather than running as a loop.

Waxed linen or polyester thread in a weight to match the existing stitching (0.8 mm or 1.0 mm is typical for bag and belt repairs). Two blunt-tipped harness needles. Beeswax block: pass the thread through the wax several times before stitching to lubricate and stiffen it. A stitching pricking iron at 3.5 to 4 mm spacing for bag leather. A stitching chisel or awl if the pricking iron is not available.

If the tear has ragged edges, trim them straight with the leatherwork knife held against a steel ruler. Do not trim more than necessary: the goal is a clean edge, not to remove leather. If the tear was in an existing stitch line, remove any broken thread from the old holes with the tip of an awl. The stitch holes are already there and can be reused if they align.

Close the tear and hold it together with bulldog clips or pegs. Mark the stitch line 3 mm in from the tear edge with the wing dividers or a silver pen. Position the pricking iron on the line and tap it through the leather with the mallet, moving along and repeating every 10 to 15 mm until the full length of the tear is marked with evenly spaced holes. Finish the holes through with the awl if the iron has not fully pierced the leather thickness.

Cut a length of waxed thread four times the length of the stitch line. Thread one needle at each end. Push one needle through the first hole from the front and pull it half the thread length through. Push the second needle through the same hole from the back. Now work along: pass the front needle through the next hole, then pass the back needle through the same hole. The back needle must pass behind the front needle's thread (not in front of it) so the threads lock at each hole. Pull both threads firmly after each stitch.

At the last hole, back-stitch two or three stitches to lock the thread. Do not knot the thread on leather: the knot will work its way through the hole over time. Instead, pull the thread ends to the back of the work, thread each needle back through the last two stitches on the back face, and cut the ends flush. The friction of the thread through the stitch loop holds the ends without a knot.

For a very thin leather (1 mm or under, such as a watch strap or fine bag gusset), reduce the stitch line to 2 mm from the edge and use a finer pricking iron at 2.5 mm spacing. A stitch that runs too close to the edge will cut through the leather margin under tension. For a curved tear, punch the stitch line on the curve in short sections, repositioning the iron to follow the curve.

Rub a small amount of leather conditioner into the repaired area after stitching. This softens any leather that the pricking iron stiffened during the work. A saddle stitch done correctly on good leather is effectively permanent: the stitch outlasts the surrounding hide and will not fail before the material does.

attrs.symptom: Thread twists or tangles between holes during stitching
attrs.cause: The thread was not waxed sufficiently, or the needles were passed the wrong way through the hole.
attrs.fix: Pull both needles fully clear of the leather after each stitch before entering the next hole. Draw each thread through the beeswax again if tangling is persistent. Check that the back needle passes behind the front thread at each hole: passing in front creates the twist.

attrs.symptom: Leather tears further along the stitch line as stitching proceeds
attrs.cause: The stitch line is too close to the edge; the leather margin between the tear and the stitching is too narrow.
attrs.fix: Stop stitching immediately. Move the stitch line 1 to 2 mm further from the tear edge and re-punch. A margin of at least 3 mm on 2 mm thick leather is the minimum for bag leather.
